name: Pecan Pralines - Prudhomme's
recipeInstructions:
1. Assemble all ingredients and utensils before starting to cook. You will need a large, heavy-bottomed aluminum pot or skillet with deep sides, a long-handled metal whisk or spoon, 2 large spoons (or an ice cream scoop with a manual release), and a lightly buttered cookie sheet.
2. Be careful not to get any of the mixture on your skin, as it sticks and can cause serious burns.
3. Melt the butter in the pot over high heat.
4. As soon as the butter is melted, add the sugars and heavy cream. Cook for 1 minute, whisking constantly.
5. Add the milk and chopped pecans. Cook for an additional 4 minutes, whisking constantly.
6. Reduce the heat to medium and continue cooking and whisking for 5 more minutes.
7. Add the pecan halves and vanilla. Continue whisking and cooking until done (see notes below for tests of doneness).
8. If the mixture starts to smoke toward the end of cooking, lower the heat.
9. Remove the pan from the heat. Quickly and carefully drop the batter onto the cookie sheet by heaping spoonfuls, using the second spoon to scoop the batter off the first (or use an ice cream scoop). Each praline should form a 2-inch patty about 1/2 inch thick.
10. Cool and store in an airtight container, or wrap each praline individually in plastic wrap or foil.
Notes:
To judge doneness, use one or more of the following guides:
1. A candy thermometer will read 240°F.
2. When done, the batter will begin forming distinct threads on the sides or bottom of the pan.
3. Near the end of the cooking time, make a test praline every few seconds. The ideal praline will not be runny and will be less shiny; when cooled, it should be opaque, lustrous, and crumbly instead of chewy.
